heroOverview: Mydriasis is an abnormally large or dilated pupil — conventionally defined as a resting diameter greater than 6 mm in normal ambient light or greater than 8 mm in complete darkness — resulting from either overactivation of the sympathetic iris dilator system or loss of parasympathetic constriction. The condition may affect one eye or both, and its clinical significance spans from entirely benign (following routine ophthalmic examination with cycloplegic drops) to immediately life-threatening (unilateral fixed mydriasis from a compressing posterior communicating artery aneurysm). A single large, unreactive pupil with ipsilateral ptosis and restricted eye movement is a neurosurgical emergency until proven otherwise. Bilateral symmetric mydriasis in an alert patient is almost always pharmacological. Ophthalmologists and neurologists together evaluate ambiguous presentations, and rapid imaging guides urgent cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Pharmacological mydriasis from iatrogenic mydriatic drops is among the most common causes, performed in millions of eye examinations annually worldwide
keyStats.demographics: Unilateral fixed mydriasis from third-nerve palsy more common in adults with vascular risk factors or connective tissue disorders; pharmacological bilateral mydriasis affects all ages
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak; aneurysmal third-nerve palsy occurs in adults 30-60; traumatic mydriasis occurs at any age after head or orbital injury
keyStats.globalCases: Posterior communicating artery aneurysm rupture affects approximately 30,000 Americans per year; many present initially with a dilated pupil

definition: Mydriasis (ICD-10: H57.04) is the condition of a persistently or pathologically dilated pupil. The term derives from the Greek word for a type of eye medication used in antiquity to widen the pupil. In clinical use, mydriasis refers specifically to a resting pupil diameter that is abnormally wide for the ambient illumination — typically above 6 mm in standard room light — and that fails to constrict appropriately to a bright light stimulus. The pupil's normal constriction to light (the direct light reflex) depends on an intact afferent arm (retina and optic nerve carrying the light signal) and an intact efferent parasympathetic arm (Edinger-Westphal nucleus in the midbrain, third cranial nerve, ciliary ganglion, and short ciliary nerves innervating the iris sphincter). Mydriasis arises whenever this efferent arc is disrupted — by compression, ischaemia, toxins, or pharmacological blockade — or whenever sympathetic drive to the dilator pupillae is pathologically elevated. Physiological mydriasis occurs normally in low light and with strong emotion or acute pain; both are expected responses. Pathological mydriasis is a large pupil that persists in bright light, is unresponsive or sluggish to the light reflex, and may be unilateral or bilateral. The critical distinction is between a pupil-involving third-nerve palsy from an expanding aneurysm — which requires emergency neurosurgery — and pharmacological mydriasis from dilating drops or systemic agents, which is benign and self-limiting.

typesClassification.description: The most common form: caused by anticholinergic drops (atropine, cyclopentolate, tropicamide) used for eye examination or uveitis treatment, or by systemic anticholinergic drugs, sympathomimetics, cocaine, amphetamines, or scopolamine patches. Bilateral, poorly reactive; resolves when the drug wears off.

Unilateral fixed dilated pupil from compression of the oculomotor nerve's outer parasympathetic fibres by a posterior communicating artery aneurysm, tentorial herniation, or other mass. Associated with ptosis and restricted eye movement. A neurosurgical emergency.

Microvascular ischaemic damage to the oculomotor nerve, as in diabetic neuropathy, typically spares the outer parasympathetic fibres and leaves the pupil relatively unaffected — a clinically important distinction from the compressive form.

Direct blunt trauma to the eye or orbit can damage the iris sphincter muscle and short ciliary nerves, producing a dilated, irregular, poorly reactive pupil. May be permanent if sphincter muscle is torn.

Postganglionic parasympathetic denervation at the ciliary ganglion producing a large pupil that reacts slowly to light but briskly to near gaze, and shows supersensitivity to dilute pilocarpine. Benign, most common in young women.

Systemic or central sympathetic overactivation from cocaine, amphetamines, acute pain, extreme anxiety, or thyrotoxicosis can bilaterally dilate pupils through excess dilator drive, though the light reflex is usually preserved.

primarySymptoms: A visibly enlarged pupil — one or both pupils appear markedly wider than expected even in bright room light where they should be small.
Failure of the affected pupil to constrict to a bright light, confirming that the dilated state is pathological rather than physiological.
Marked light sensitivity (photophobia) in the eye with a dilated pupil, because an impaired iris sphincter cannot regulate the amount of light entering the eye.
Blurred near vision in the affected eye, as a paralysed ciliary muscle cannot accommodate to near objects when parasympathetic function is lost.
Double vision (diplopia) when a dilated pupil accompanies a third-nerve palsy, because the eye cannot move normally — it drifts outward and downward.
Ipsilateral drooping of the upper eyelid (ptosis) in third-nerve palsy, since the levator palpebrae superioris is also supplied by the oculomotor nerve.
A headache described as the worst ever experienced — an ominous signal when it accompanies a fixed dilated pupil, suggesting subarachnoid haemorrhage from an aneurysm.
Blurred distance vision bilaterally when pharmacological cycloplegic drops paralyse both ciliary muscles simultaneously, temporarily preventing far focus.
Iris irregularity, vermiform movement under slit-lamp examination, and variable near-light dissociation in Adie's tonic pupil.
Halo vision or glare sensitivity in bright environments from the over-dilated pupil admitting excessive light and creating spherical aberration.
earlyWarningSigns: A newly enlarged pupil on one side noticed in a mirror or photograph when this was not previously present.
Increasing sensitivity to bright light or difficulty reducing glare in one eye accompanied by a visibly wider pupil.
A slight droop of the upper eyelid appearing on the same side as the larger pupil, which may represent early third-nerve compression.
Onset of headache — even mild — at the same time a pupil is first noticed to be enlarged: this combination warrants same-day specialist evaluation.
emergencySigns: A single fixed dilated pupil that does not respond to bright light, accompanied by the worst headache ever experienced — suspect ruptured intracranial aneurysm; call emergency services immediately.

Dilated unreactive pupil with a drooping eyelid and inability to move the eye normally (it looks outward and downward) — third-nerve palsy from an aneurysm until proven otherwise; emergency brain imaging required.
New fixed dilated pupil after head trauma — intracranial haemorrhage with herniation until proven otherwise; emergency services immediately.
Rapidly worsening headache with a progressively dilating pupil in a previously alert patient — consider expanding intracranial mass lesion or threatened herniation.
Bilateral large fixed pupils with agitation, tachycardia, and dry flushed skin — anticholinergic toxidrome from drug overdose or plant poisoning; emergency toxicological management.

causes.cause: Pharmacological blockade of the iris sphincter (anticholinergic agents)
causes.description: Topical cycloplegic drops — atropine, cyclopentolate, tropicamide, homatropine — are the most common cause of mydriasis encountered clinically, used routinely for fundus examination and uveitis treatment. Systemic anticholinergics (scopolamine, antihistamines, tricyclics), cocaine, amphetamines, and accidental belladonna alkaloid exposure produce bilateral mydriasis. The pupil is poorly reactive; pilocarpine 1% does not constrict it, distinguishing pharmacological blockade from neurological mydriasis.

causes.cause: Compressive third cranial nerve palsy from intracranial aneurysm
causes.description: The parasympathetic fibres of the oculomotor nerve run on its dorsomedial surface and are the first to be stretched when a posterior communicating artery aneurysm expands. Even a small aneurysm or a sentinel bleed can produce a fixed dilated pupil before the aneurysm ruptures and causes subarachnoid haemorrhage. This is the most dangerous cause of unilateral mydriasis.

causes.cause: Tentorial herniation from raised intracranial pressure
causes.description: When intracranial pressure rises acutely from haemorrhage, infarction, or oedema, the medial temporal lobe herniates downward through the tentorium cerebelli, compressing the third cranial nerve on that side. The ipsilateral pupil dilates and becomes unreactive — Hutchinson's pupil — a critical sign of impending brainstem compression.

Inflammatory or immune damage to the ciliary ganglion destroys the postganglionic parasympathetic neurons that constrict the pupil and accommodate the lens. The result is a chronically dilated, sluggishly reactive pupil in an otherwise well young adult. Denervation supersensitivity means dilute pilocarpine 0.1% constricts an Adie pupil but not a pharmacologically blocked one.

Direct impact to the eye can tear the iris sphincter muscle fibres and damage the short ciliary nerves, producing traumatic iridoplegia — a dilated, irregular, poorly reactive pupil. Associated findings include subconjunctival haemorrhage, iris tears, and lens dislocation. The sphincter tear may be permanent.

Drugs that release noradrenaline or block its reuptake — cocaine, methamphetamine, phenylephrine, ephedrine — activate the iris dilator and produce bilateral, symmetrically dilated pupils. The light reflex is typically preserved, distinguishing this from parasympathetic lesions, and both pupils constrict to pilocarpine.

A mid-dilated (around 4-6 mm), poorly reactive pupil can appear during an acute attack of angle-closure glaucoma. The pupil dilation is caused by ischaemia of the iris sphincter from the sudden pressure rise and by autonomic reflex responses to acute pain. It is accompanied by severe eye pain, nausea, a red eye, and a hazy cornea — a package that distinguishes it from neurological mydriasis.

riskFactors.factor: Routine ophthalmic examination with dilating drops
riskFactors.description: Cycloplegic or mydriatic eye drops are used in virtually all comprehensive eye examinations for fundus viewing; pharmacological mydriasis following an examination is entirely expected and resolves in a few hours.

riskFactors.factor: Intracranial aneurysm or connective tissue disorder
riskFactors.description: Posterior communicating artery aneurysm causes the most dangerous unilateral mydriasis. Risk factors include a family history of aneurysm, polycystic kidney disease, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Marfan syndrome, and smoking. Annual risk of rupture rises sharply with aneurysm diameter above 7 mm.

Blunt orbital or head trauma directly damages the iris sphincter (traumatic iridoplegia) or raises intracranial pressure leading to tentorial herniation with oculomotor nerve compression. Traumatic mydriasis is a common finding after significant ocular injury.

Sympathomimetic drugs reliably produce bilateral mydriasis through excess noradrenergic activation of the iris dilator. While the resulting mydriasis is pharmacological and reversible, it can cause clinically significant photophobia and glare sensitivity.

Diabetic microvascular disease can cause ischaemic third-nerve palsy; crucially, the pupil is typically spared or mildly affected in the diabetic form, unlike the aneurysmal form where the pupil is fixed and widely dilated. Distinguishing these two presentations is essential.

riskFactors.factor: Raised intracranial pressure from any cause
riskFactors.description: Any mass lesion, haemorrhage, or cerebral oedema causing significant elevation of intracranial pressure can produce tentorial herniation with ipsilateral third-nerve compression and a dilated unreactive pupil. Early recognition is critical to prevent irreversible brainstem damage.

Systemic anticholinergics — tricyclic antidepressants, antihistamines, antispasmodics, scopolamine patches — and incidental contact with jimsonweed (Datura), belladonna, or henbane can cause bilateral pharmacological mydriasis. Medication review identifies these causes.

affectedDemographics: Patients undergoing ophthalmic examination with mydriatic drops — the largest numerically, but an entirely benign pharmacological form
Young women aged 20-40, in whom Adie's tonic pupil is most prevalent
Middle-aged adults with intracranial aneurysm risk factors (smoking, hypertension, family history, connective tissue disease)
People who have sustained blunt ocular or head trauma at any age
Adults using recreational stimulants (cocaine, methamphetamine) who develop bilateral pharmacological mydriasis
Patients on anticholinergic medications — a large and growing population among older adults on polypharmacy
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis starts with establishing whether mydriasis is unilateral or bilateral, and whether the pupil reacts to bright light. Bilateral symmetric mydriasis with a preserved light reflex in an alert patient almost always reflects pharmacological stimulant use or systemic sympathomimetic effect; a medication and drug history is the first step. Bilateral mydriasis with an absent or very sluggish light reflex in a symptomatic patient suggests bilateral parasympathetic blockade from anticholinergics. Unilateral mydriasis is the scenario that requires most urgent assessment. The critical initial question is whether the light reflex is absent (suggesting a lesion of the parasympathetic arc — the efferent pupil defect) or present (suggesting the afferent arc or pharmacological cause). A unilateral fixed dilated pupil with ptosis and ophthalmoplegia demands immediate CT angiography or MRI angiography to exclude a posterior communicating artery aneurysm, which is a neurosurgical emergency. If imaging is negative, the next steps include pharmacological testing with pilocarpine: 1% pilocarpine will not constrict a pharmacologically blocked pupil but will constrict an Adie's pupil or a compressed third-nerve palsy pupil (at higher concentrations). Dilute pilocarpine (0.1%) constricts an Adie's pupil through denervation supersensitivity. Slit-lamp examination assesses the iris for sphincter tears from trauma, and tonometry excludes glaucoma. In the trauma context, a dilated pupil requires CT head to exclude intracranial haemorrhage before attributing it to direct iris damage.

diagnosticTests.test: Pupillary light reflex testing (direct and consensual)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines whether the dilated pupil has a functioning afferent pathway (the light reflex is present) or an efferent defect (no constriction). A fixed dilated pupil that does not constrict even when the opposite eye is illuminated confirms a lesion of the efferent parasympathetic arc.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician shines a bright light into each eye in turn and observes constriction in both the illuminated eye (direct reflex) and the fellow eye (consensual reflex); takes about 2-3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes pharmacological blockade from neurological mydriasis. Dilute pilocarpine 0.1% constricts Adie's pupil through denervation supersensitivity but not a normal pupil or a pharmacologically blocked one. Pilocarpine 1% constricts a neurologically denervated pupil but fails to constrict a pharmacologically blocked pupil.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Eye drops are instilled and pupils re-examined after 30-45 minutes; the test is painless and the distinction is clear.

diagnosticTests.test: CT angiography or MRI angiography of the circle of Willis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Emergency exclusion of a posterior communicating artery aneurysm causing compressive third-nerve palsy. CT angiography is the fastest and most sensitive modality in the acute setting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT angiography takes 5-10 minutes and requires an intravenous contrast injection; MRI angiography takes 20-30 minutes. Both are painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural iris sphincter tears from ocular trauma, vermiform iris movements in Adie's pupil, anterior chamber inflammation from uveitis, and shallow anterior chamber angle suggesting glaucoma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A focused slit beam illuminates the iris and anterior chamber while you rest on the lamp support; takes about 10-15 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes acute angle-closure glaucoma as the cause of a mid-dilated, poorly reactive pupil in a patient with eye pain, nausea, and a red eye; pressure above 21 mmHg confirms raised IOP.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe or puff of air briefly touches the surface of the eye; takes under 1 minute. A topical anaesthetic drop is used for contact tonometry.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for diabetes (ischaemic third-nerve palsy that typically spares the pupil) and vasculitis or temporal arteritis as causes of oculomotor nerve ischaemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; results available within hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is entirely directed at the underlying cause. Pharmacological mydriasis — the most common form — requires no treatment other than avoiding bright light until the drops wear off; short-acting agents such as tropicamide resolve in 4-6 hours, while atropine can last 7-14 days. Adie's tonic pupil is benign; if photophobia or near-vision difficulty is significant, dilute pilocarpine 0.1% eye drops reduce the pupil size by exploiting denervation supersensitivity, and reading glasses compensate for accommodative insufficiency. Anticholinergic toxidrome from drug ingestion or plant poisoning causing bilateral mydriasis with agitation, tachycardia, and dry flushed skin is managed with physostigmine in selected cases, supportive sedation with benzodiazepines, and intensive monitoring; pilocarpine is not effective orally and is not the treatment of choice for systemic toxicity. Acute angle-closure glaucoma with a mid-dilated pupil requires immediate intraocular pressure lowering with topical beta-blockers, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, and pilocarpine followed by laser peripheral iridotomy. Third-nerve palsy from a posterior communicating artery aneurysm is a neurosurgical emergency requiring endovascular coiling or surgical clipping performed as rapidly as possible to prevent rupture; the earlier the intervention, the better the outcome for the nerve and for life. Ischaemic third-nerve palsy from diabetes is managed conservatively with glycaemic optimisation, blood pressure control, and statin therapy, with close monitoring for pupillary change that would suggest a compressive cause has been missed. Traumatic iridoplegia requires no specific treatment; the pupil dilator-sphincter imbalance is observed, with cosmetic effects managed by tinted contact lenses if the anisocoria is bothersome. In tentorial herniation, emergency management of raised intracranial pressure — mannitol, hyperventilation, and urgent neurosurgery — takes priority over the pupillary finding itself.

medicalTreatments.name: Dilute pilocarpine 0.1% eye drops for Adie's tonic pupil
medicalTreatments.description: Exploits denervation supersensitivity of the denervated iris sphincter to constrict the dilated pupil and partially restore accommodation. Reduces photophobia and the cosmetic size difference. Has no effect on normal pupils or pharmacologically blocked pupils at this concentration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces clinically visible pupil constriction in approximately 80% of confirmed Adie's tonic pupils; normal pupils remain unaffected at this concentration.

medicalTreatments.name: Endovascular coiling or surgical clipping for aneurysmal third-nerve palsy
medicalTreatments.description: Emergency obliteration of the posterior communicating artery aneurysm compressing the third cranial nerve. Endovascular coiling packs the aneurysm sac with platinum coils under fluoroscopic guidance; surgical clipping places a titanium clip at the aneurysm neck via craniotomy. Pupil and eye movement recovery occurs gradually over 3-12 months post-treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Aneurysm occlusion in approximately 90-95% of cases with modern endovascular techniques; pupil recovery occurs in 60-80% of patients within 12 months of treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Intraocular pressure-lowering therapy for acute angle-closure glaucoma
medicalTreatments.description: Combination of topical timolol, brimonidine, apraclonidine, and acetazolamide (systemic) rapidly lowers IOP to protect the optic nerve and restore iris sphincter function. Once IOP is controlled, laser peripheral iridotomy or surgical iridectomy prevents recurrence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IOP is lowered to safe levels within 1-2 hours in approximately 85-90% of acute angle-closure attacks with combined medical therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Glycaemic control and vascular optimisation for diabetic ischaemic third-nerve palsy
medicalTreatments.description: Optimising HbA1c, blood pressure, and statin therapy improves the microvascular environment and supports nerve recovery. The pupil is usually spared in the diabetic form; if it becomes involved, this should prompt urgent repeat imaging to exclude a co-existing aneurysm.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Spontaneous recovery of diplopia and ptosis occurs in approximately 75-90% of diabetic ischaemic third-nerve palsies within 3-6 months with metabolic optimisation.

medicalTreatments.name: Physostigmine or benzodiazepines for anticholinergic toxidrome
medicalTreatments.description: Physostigmine, a reversible cholinesterase inhibitor, can reverse severe anticholinergic toxicity including dilated pupils, agitation, tachycardia, and hyperthermia when the clinical picture is clearly anticholinergic. Benzodiazepines manage agitation and seizures. Both require close monitoring in an emergency setting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Physostigmine reverses central and peripheral anticholinergic effects in approximately 85% of patients with a clear cholinergic deficit when used appropriately by experienced emergency physicians.

surgicalOptions.name: Endovascular coiling of intracranial aneurysm
surgicalOptions.description: Platinum coils are deployed inside the aneurysm sac via a catheter advanced from the groin to the cerebral circulation. The coils induce thrombosis, excluding the aneurysm from the circulation. Preferred at most centres for posterior communicating artery aneurysms because of lower procedural morbidity than open surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete or near-complete occlusion in approximately 90% of cases; retreatment required in approximately 20% of coiled aneurysms over 10 years due to coil compaction.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical clipping of intracranial aneurysm
surgicalOptions.description: A titanium clip is placed across the aneurysm neck via craniotomy, excluding it from the circulation. Preferred when the aneurysm anatomy is unfavourable for coiling or in young patients who need a durable cure. More invasive than coiling.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete occlusion in approximately 95% of suitable cases; lower long-term recurrence rate than coiling.

surgicalOptions.name: Laser peripheral iridotomy for angle-closure glaucoma
surgicalOptions.description: A laser creates a small hole through the peripheral iris, bypassing the pupil block mechanism and allowing aqueous humour to equalize pressure between the anterior and posterior chambers. Prevents future acute attacks and can be performed in both eyes prophylactically.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Prevents recurrent acute angle-closure attacks in approximately 90% of eyes with pupil-block mechanism confirmed.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dark or tinted spectacles for photophobia from mydriasis
alternativeTreatments.description: Good-quality photochromic or polarised lenses reduce glare and light sensitivity in patients with Adie's tonic pupil, residual pharmacological mydriasis, or traumatic iridoplegia. They do not treat the underlying condition but significantly improve comfort and function.

alternativeTreatments.name: Prism spectacles or occlusion patch for diplopia during third-nerve recovery
alternativeTreatments.description: Fresnel prisms applied to one lens or occlusion of one eye with a frosted lens eliminates double vision during the 3-6 month recovery period of ischaemic third-nerve palsy, allowing normal daily function without the distress of constant diplopia.

whySeeSpecialist: Any new-onset unilateral fixed dilated pupil, particularly with ptosis or double vision, requires same-day emergency evaluation to exclude a compressing intracranial aneurysm before it ruptures. An ophthalmologist evaluates Adie's pupil, traumatic iridoplegia, and angle-closure glaucoma; a neurologist or neurosurgeon handles third-nerve palsy, herniation syndromes, and intracranial aneurysm. Bilateral mydriasis without neurological signs is evaluated by the clinician managing the drug or toxin exposure.
doctorSelectionGuide: A neuro-ophthalmologist combines the skills needed to evaluate pupil-involving third-nerve palsy, Adie's pupil, and traumatic iridoplegia. For aneurysm, access to interventional neuroradiology and neurosurgery at the same centre is essential. Acute angle-closure glaucoma needs a retina or glaucoma specialist with laser capability. Anticholinergic toxidrome is managed by emergency toxicology. For routine post-examination pharmacological mydriasis, reassurance from the examining optometrist or ophthalmologist is all that is needed.
preventionStrategies: Inform all eye examination patients about expected pharmacological mydriasis after cycloplegic drops and advise them not to drive for 4-8 hours after tropicamide or up to 48 hours after cyclopentolate.
Control modifiable aneurysm risk factors — smoking cessation, blood pressure management — and screen first-degree relatives of patients with a ruptured intracranial aneurysm.
Wear appropriate eye protection during contact sports, industrial work, and activities with risk of blunt orbital trauma.
Wash hands after handling scopolamine motion-sickness patches or any plant material from the nightshade family to prevent accidental anticholinergic mydriasis.
Optimise blood glucose and cardiovascular risk factors to reduce the incidence of diabetic ischaemic third-nerve palsy.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid driving immediately after ophthalmic examination with mydriatic drops; arrange transport in advance.
Wear sunglasses outdoors if living with Adie's tonic pupil or any chronic mydriasis, as the enlarged pupil cannot protect the retina from bright sunlight.
Avoid stimulant drugs, which cause bilateral mydriasis and add unnecessary photophobia and glare sensitivity.
Maintain blood pressure at target and adhere to antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy if prescribed for vascular protection.
Report any change in the degree of pupil dilation, new ptosis, or diplopia promptly to avoid missing evolution of a treatable underlying cause.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet for cardiovascular and vascular health, reducing aneurysm and stroke risk
Low-sodium foods to control blood pressure and reduce aneurysm growth risk
Adequate hydration, particularly important during recovery from raised intracranial pressure events
Foods high in antioxidants and omega-3 fatty acids to support neurological and vascular recovery
avoid: Tobacco and nicotine in all forms, which are among the strongest modifiable risk factors for intracranial aneurysm formation and rupture
Excessive alcohol, which raises blood pressure and increases haemorrhagic stroke risk
High-sodium and trans-fat-rich foods that accelerate hypertension and atherosclerosis
Stimulant drugs or supplements containing ephedrine or pseudoephedrine, which can acutely raise blood pressure and trigger aneurysm rupture

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise is beneficial for vascular health. Patients with an unruptured intracranial aneurysm should follow their neurovascular team's guidance — high-intensity activities that markedly raise blood pressure or require Valsalva manoeuvres may be restricted until the aneurysm is treated. After aneurysm repair, graded return to exercise over 4-8 weeks is typical. Patients with Adie's tonic pupil, pharmacological mydriasis, or traumatic iridoplegia have no exercise restrictions beyond avoiding bright unprotected light exposure.
dailyManagement: If living with Adie's tonic pupil, use dilute pilocarpine drops as prescribed and wear photochromic spectacles outdoors.
Carry a note documenting known Adie's pupil to prevent unnecessary emergency investigations by clinicians who notice the asymmetric pupils.
Attend scheduled follow-up imaging if you have an unruptured intracranial aneurysm; aneurysm surveillance with MRI or CT angiography is typically performed at 6-12 month intervals.
Adhere to all prescribed antihypertensive, antiplatelet, and lipid-lowering medications to protect vascular health.
Report any new headache, ptosis, or change in pupil reactivity to your neurologist or ophthalmologist without delay.
prognosis: Prognosis depends entirely on the cause. Pharmacological mydriasis from cycloplegic drops resolves fully within hours to days without any lasting effect. Adie's tonic pupil is a permanent but benign finding; near vision difficulty can be managed with reading glasses and dilute pilocarpine. Traumatic iridoplegia from sphincter tears is usually permanent, though the functional impact is mild in most patients. Ischaemic third-nerve palsy from diabetes recovers fully or nearly fully in approximately 75-90% of patients within 3-6 months. Aneurysmal third-nerve palsy treated emergently before rupture has an excellent surgical outcome and good long-term nerve recovery; ruptured aneurysm with subarachnoid haemorrhage carries a 30-day mortality of approximately 40% and significant neurological morbidity in survivors. Acute angle-closure glaucoma treated promptly has good visual outcomes in most cases; a delay of more than 24-48 hours risks permanent optic nerve damage.
recoveryTimeline: Tropicamide pharmacological mydriasis resolves in 4-6 hours; cyclopentolate in 24-48 hours; atropine in 7-14 days. Adie's pupil does not resolve but adapts well with management. Diabetic ischaemic third-nerve palsy begins to improve at 4-8 weeks and resolves in 3-6 months. After aneurysm coiling or clipping, pupil and eye movement recovery follows over 3-12 months. Angle-closure glaucoma pupil returns to normal after successful IOP lowering within hours to days.
complications: Subarachnoid haemorrhage from rupture of the aneurysm causing mydriasis — the most catastrophic complication, with approximately 40% 30-day mortality and significant neurological disability in survivors.
Permanent photophobia and visual glare from a chronically dilated pupil unable to reduce incoming light, affecting quality of life in Adie's pupil and traumatic iridoplegia.
Permanent diplopia and ptosis if a third-nerve palsy does not recover fully, requiring long-term prism spectacles or corrective strabismus surgery.
Irreversible optic nerve damage from acute angle-closure glaucoma if the dilated pupil presentation causes diagnostic delay in recognising and treating the pressure rise.

Anticholinergic toxidrome complications — hyperthermia, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, and cardiac arrhythmia — when bilateral pharmacological mydriasis results from systemic drug overdose rather than topical application.

supportResources.name: Brain Aneurysm Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient and family resources on intracranial aneurysm detection, treatment, and recovery, the most dangerous cause of unilateral fixed mydriasis.

supportResources.name: NINDS — Cerebral Aneurysm
supportResources.description: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke patient information on brain aneurysm, symptoms, treatment, and clinical trials.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Dilated Eye Exam
supportResources.description: Patient information on pharmacological dilation during eye examination, the most common benign cause of mydriasis.

sources.title: Thompson HS, Pilley SF. Unequal pupils. A flow chart for sorting out the anisocorias. Survey of Ophthalmology, 1976
Jacobson DM. Pupil involvement in patients with diabetes-associated oculomotor nerve palsy. Archives of Ophthalmology, 1998
Wiebers DO et al. Unruptured intracranial aneurysms: natural history, clinical outcome, and risks of surgical and endovascular treatment. Lancet, 2003
Molyneux AJ et al. International Subarachnoid Aneurysm Trial (ISAT). Lancet, 2002

confusedWithConditions.name: Anisocoria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anisocoria describes the size difference between pupils; mydriasis specifies the dilated state of one or both pupils. Mydriasis of one eye is a common cause of anisocoria but they are not the same: both pupils can be dilated (bilateral mydriasis) without any anisocoria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Miosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Miosis is abnormally small pupils; mydriasis is abnormally large pupils. They share the same autonomic pupillomotor system but represent opposite lesions — parasympathetic excess or sympathetic deficiency for miosis, and parasympathetic deficiency or sympathetic excess for mydriasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute angle-closure glaucoma produces a mid-dilated, semi-fixed pupil from iris sphincter ischaemia, which can be confused with neurological mydriasis. It is distinguished by markedly elevated intraocular pressure, severe eye pain, a red eye, and a hazy cornea — not features of neurological pupil dilation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Advanced cataracts do not cause mydriasis; however, dense posterior subcapsular cataracts can impair the light reaching the retina, reducing the afferent drive for pupil constriction and making the light reflex sluggish, which can superficially mimic a relative pupillary dilation.

relatedConditions.name: Anisocoria
relatedConditions.relevance: Unilateral mydriasis is one of the major causes of anisocoria; the evaluation of a dilated pupil and an unequal pupil overlap substantially.

relatedConditions.name: Miosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Miosis and mydriasis represent opposite ends of the same pupillary control system; understanding one requires understanding the other.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute angle-closure glaucoma produces a semi-dilated pupil and enters the emergency differential diagnosis of mydriasis; glaucoma therapy with mydriatics requires monitoring for angle narrowing.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Ophthalmoplegic migraine can produce transient third-nerve involvement with pupil dilation; cluster headache autonomic features include alternating miosis and mydriasis during attacks.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Demyelinating lesions can affect the third cranial nerve or its nucleus and produce an internuclear ophthalmoplegia with variable pupillary effects including relative mydriasis.

faqs.question: What is mydriasis?
faqs.answer: Mydriasis is an abnormally large, dilated pupil that does not constrict normally in bright light. The pupil may be dilated in one eye or both. It ranges from completely harmless — after routine eye drops — to life-threatening, when it signals a brain aneurysm compressing the nerve that controls the pupil.

faqs.question: What causes dilated pupils?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is deliberate dilation with eye drops during an eye examination. Other causes include certain systemic medications, cocaine or amphetamines, an Adie's tonic pupil (benign nerve damage), blunt eye trauma, acute angle-closure glaucoma, and — most dangerously — a brain aneurysm pressing on the third cranial nerve. Stimulant drugs dilate both pupils.

faqs.question: When is a dilated pupil an emergency?
faqs.answer: A single fixed dilated pupil that does not respond to light, especially with a drooping eyelid and double vision or the worst headache ever, is a medical emergency. Call emergency services immediately — this combination can mean a brain aneurysm is about to rupture or has already ruptured. Do not wait to see if it improves.

faqs.question: How long do dilated pupils from eye drops last?
faqs.answer: It depends on the drop used. Tropicamide, used in routine eye examinations, wears off in 4 to 6 hours. Cyclopentolate lasts 24 to 48 hours. Atropine, the longest-acting, can cause dilation for 7 to 14 days. You should avoid driving and exposure to bright light during this period.

faqs.question: Can drugs cause dilated pupils?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cocaine, methamphetamine, MDMA, and other stimulants reliably dilate both pupils by activating the sympathetic nervous system. Anticholinergic drugs — including some antihistamines, tricyclic antidepressants, and scopolamine patches — also dilate pupils by blocking the iris sphincter. Both causes produce bilateral dilation with preserved or sluggish light reflexes.

faqs.question: What is Adie's tonic pupil?
faqs.answer: Adie's tonic pupil is a large pupil that reacts very slowly to bright light but constricts readily when you focus on something close. It results from damage to the ciliary ganglion nerve and is benign. It is most common in young women and does not indicate a dangerous underlying condition. Low-dose pilocarpine drops can reduce the pupil size.

faqs.question: Why does a brain aneurysm cause a dilated pupil?
faqs.answer: The parasympathetic fibres that constrict the pupil run on the outer surface of the third cranial nerve. When a posterior communicating artery aneurysm expands, it stretches these outer fibres first, abolishing the constriction signal and leaving the pupil dilated and fixed. This is why a new fixed dilated pupil with a headache is treated as an aneurysm emergency.

faqs.question: What is the difference between mydriasis and anisocoria?
faqs.answer: Mydriasis means a dilated pupil — one or both may be abnormally large. Anisocoria means a size difference between the two pupils. A dilated pupil in one eye causes anisocoria, but both pupils can be dilated without any anisocoria. Mydriasis describes the absolute state; anisocoria describes the relative difference.

faqs.question: Can glaucoma cause a dilated pupil?
faqs.answer: Acute angle-closure glaucoma can cause a mid-dilated, poorly reactive pupil because the sudden pressure rise damages the iris sphincter. Unlike a neurological dilated pupil, it comes with severe eye pain, a red eye, nausea, and a hazy cornea. It needs emergency treatment to lower the eye pressure before optic nerve damage occurs.

faqs.question: Does a dilated pupil mean a concussion?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. After a head injury, a dilated or unresponsive pupil can signal serious intracranial bleeding or brain herniation — not just a concussion. Any new unequal or non-reactive pupil after head trauma needs immediate emergency assessment. A mild concussion typically does not produce a fixed dilated pupil.

faqs.question: How is a dilated pupil from an aneurysm treated?
faqs.answer: Emergency endovascular coiling or neurosurgical clipping of the aneurysm. A catheter-based coiling procedure inserts platinum coils into the aneurysm to seal it off; surgery clips the aneurysm neck through a craniotomy. Both aim to prevent rupture. Pupil and eye movement recovery then follows over 3 to 12 months.

faqs.question: Is mydriasis always a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: No. The most common cause by far is pharmacological dilation from routine eye examination drops, which is completely harmless. Adie's pupil and traumatic iridoplegia are benign chronic conditions. Serious causes — aneurysm, herniation, organophosphate toxicity, anticholinergic overdose — are accompanied by other symptoms that guide urgency.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause dilated pupils?
faqs.answer: Acute anxiety or fear triggers sympathetic arousal, which transiently dilates both pupils through increased iris dilator activity. This is a normal autonomic response that resolves as soon as the anxiety passes. Persistent dilated pupils unrelated to emotional state are not caused by anxiety and need clinical evaluation.

faqs.question: What is the pilocarpine test and how does it work?
faqs.answer: The pilocarpine test uses eye drops to distinguish the cause of a dilated pupil. Dilute pilocarpine 0.1% constricts an Adie's pupil through denervation supersensitivity but leaves normal or pharmacologically blocked pupils unchanged. Pilocarpine 1% constricts a neurologically damaged pupil but cannot overcome pharmacological blockade with atropine.

faqs.question: Why does my pupil stay dilated after using an eye dilating drop?
faqs.answer: Pupil dilation drops block the muscarinic receptor on the iris sphincter, preventing acetylcholine from constricting the pupil. Short-acting drops like tropicamide wear off in 4 to 6 hours; longer-acting ones can take days. During this time, bright light is uncomfortable and near vision is blurred because the ciliary muscle is also paralysed. Both effects are temporary.

faqs.question: Can a dilated pupil affect vision?
faqs.answer: Yes. A dilated pupil admits too much light, causing photophobia and glare sensitivity. It also admits light through the lens periphery, which creates optical aberrations that blur vision. When a cycloplegic drug is also present, near focus is additionally impaired. These effects resolve as the drug wears off; chronic conditions like Adie's pupil can be managed with tinted lenses.

faqs.question: Is mydriasis the same as a blown pupil?
faqs.answer: The term 'blown pupil' is used in emergency medicine to describe a fixed, maximally dilated, non-reactive pupil as a sign of third-nerve compression from brain herniation or aneurysm. It is a severe form of mydriasis. Not all mydriasis is a 'blown pupil' — pharmacological dilation from drops is mydriasis but not a blown pupil.

faqs.question: Can migraines cause dilated pupils?
faqs.answer: Classic migraine does not typically cause a dilated pupil. Ophthalmoplegic migraine (now considered a cranial neuropathy) can produce third-nerve involvement with pupil dilation during or after attacks. Cluster headaches, related to migraines, can cause transient eye changes including sometimes a dilated pupil on the affected side during an attack.

faqs.question: How do I know if my dilated pupil is from eye drops or something serious?
faqs.answer: If you recently had an eye examination or used any eye drops, or handled a scopolamine patch, pharmacological dilation is almost certain. If no drugs or drops are involved and the dilation appeared suddenly with headache, neck pain, drooping eyelid, or double vision, seek emergency care immediately. A clinician can confirm the cause with a simple pharmacological test using dilute pilocarpine drops.

faqs.question: What happens if a third-nerve palsy from an aneurysm is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, the aneurysm causing the third-nerve palsy can rupture and cause subarachnoid haemorrhage, which carries approximately 40% 30-day mortality and significant neurological disability in survivors. The warning sign of a dilated pupil represents an opportunity to treat the aneurysm electively, before rupture, with far better outcomes.

metaDescription: Mydriasis is an abnormally dilated pupil that can signal a brain aneurysm, Adie's pupil, or pharmacological dilation. Learn causes, emergencies, and treatment.
